schools.summary: Gill: this was said in answer to Moses' objection from his own meanness and want of ready speech; God made him a god to Pharaoh, that is, to act in his name with power and authority; the witnesses note God telling Moses he had made him a god to Pharaoh, and Aaron his brother should be his prophet — the great dignity put on Moses, to stand before Pharaoh as God's representative (God answering Moses' fear by raising him to a high office: 'See, I have made thee a god to Pharaoh: and Aaron thy brother shall be thy prophet'; the meaning of 'a god to Pharaoh,' not that Moses was made divine but that he should stand before Pharaoh 'in God's stead,' clothed with God's authority and power, to command and to judge, so that Pharaoh should fear him as one bearing the word and might of God; the appointing of Aaron, 'Aaron thy brother shall be thy prophet,' that, as a prophet speaks for God, so Aaron should speak for Moses, declaring to Pharaoh what Moses received from God, answering Moses' plea of slow speech; the order of God's working, that Moses should be to Aaron as God and Aaron to Pharaoh as the prophet; the honour God puts on his servants, making them his ambassadors).

debates.question: What does it mean that Moses was made 'a god to Pharaoh'?

sides.position: Not that Moses was made divine, but that he should stand before Pharaoh in God's stead, clothed with God's authority and power to command and to judge — Pharaoh fearing him as one bearing the word and might of God

themes: See, I have made thee a god to Pharaoh
and Aaron thy brother shall be thy prophet
Moses set in God's stead, clothed with his authority before Pharaoh
Aaron his prophet to speak for him, the honour God puts on his servants as ambassadors

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: Moses was to speak all that God commanded, and Aaron to speak to Pharaoh that he let Israel go; the witnesses note God charging that Moses speak all he commanded, and Aaron speak it to Pharaoh, that he send Israel out — the order of the message and its one demand, the release of God's people (God fixing the channel and the substance of the word: 'Thou shalt speak all that I command thee: and Aaron thy brother shall speak unto Pharaoh, that he send the children of Israel out of his land'; the strict charge, 'Thou shalt speak all that I command thee,' that Moses was to deliver God's whole message, neither more nor less, a faithful steward of the word committed to him; the chain of speaking, that Moses should receive from God and tell Aaron, and 'Aaron... shall speak unto Pharaoh,' the appointed mouthpiece; the one demand of the message, 'that he send the children of Israel out of his land,' the plain and constant requirement, that Pharaoh let God's people go; the faithfulness required of God's messengers, to speak all his word and nothing of their own).

themes: Thou shalt speak all that I command thee
that he send the children of Israel out of his land
Moses to speak all God commanded, Aaron to carry it to Pharaoh
the one demand of Israel's release, the messengers faithful to God's word

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: 'I will harden Pharaoh's heart' means God would permit his stubbornness and obstinacy still to remain, that he might have the greater opportunity of showing his wonders; the witnesses note God saying he would harden Pharaoh's heart and multiply his signs and wonders in Egypt — the foretelling of Pharaoh's hardening and the multiplied judgments (God declaring beforehand how he will deal: 'I will harden Pharaoh's heart, and multiply my signs and my wonders in the land of Egypt'; the much-debated phrase, 'I will harden Pharaoh's heart,' that the commentators take it variously, some with Clarke as God's permitting and leaving Pharaoh to his own stubbornness and obstinacy, withdrawing restraint and giving him up to the hardness he chose, others as a judicial hardening, God justly delivering up to greater blindness one who had first hardened his own heart; that all agree the sin and the hardness were Pharaoh's own, he is said to harden his own heart, yet that God, in righteous judgment, was over it, ordering it to his own ends; the purpose, 'and multiply my signs and my wonders,' that Pharaoh's continued resistance gave occasion for God to multiply his mighty wonders, the more to display his power and glory; that God overrules even the hardness of the wicked to the praise of his name).

debates.question: In what sense does God 'harden Pharaoh's heart'?

sides.position: By permission — God leaves Pharaoh to his own chosen stubbornness and obstinacy, withdrawing restraint, that he may have the greater opportunity to show his wonders

By judicial hardening — God justly delivers up to greater blindness one who had first hardened his own heart; yet all agree the hardness was Pharaoh's own (he hardens his own heart, 8:15), God in righteous judgment over it

themes: And I will harden Pharaoh's heart
and multiply my signs and my wonders in the land of Egypt
the foretold hardening, the hardness Pharaoh's own yet under God's judgment
Pharaoh's resistance occasioning the multiplied display of God's power and glory

schools.summary: Gill: Pharaoh would not regard what they said, nor obey God's command by them; this God apprised them of beforehand; the witnesses note God declaring that Pharaoh would not hearken, that he might lay his hand on Egypt and bring out his people by great judgments — the foreknowledge of refusal and the purpose of mighty judgment (God showing the end of Pharaoh's hardness: 'Pharaoh shall not hearken unto you, that I may lay my hand upon Egypt, and bring forth mine armies... by great judgments'; the warning of refusal, 'Pharaoh shall not hearken unto you,' foretold that Moses and Aaron might not be cast down when he despised their message; the divine purpose served, 'that I may lay my hand upon Egypt,' that Pharaoh's obstinacy gave God occasion to lay his hand, his mighty power, in judgment on Egypt; the calling of Israel 'mine armies, and my people,' that the despised slaves were God's own hosts whom he would bring forth out of the land of Egypt; the manner, 'by great judgments,' that the deliverance should come not quietly but with great and terrible judgments on Egypt, the more to glorify God and humble the proud; that God's purpose stands sure through the very rebellion of his enemies).

themes: But Pharaoh shall not hearken unto you, that I may lay my hand upon Egypt
by great judgments
God foretelling Pharaoh's refusal and his purpose of mighty judgment
the despised slaves God's own armies, his purpose sure through the rebellion of his enemies

schools.summary: Gill: the Egyptians should know that he is Jehovah, the one only true and living God, by the judgments executed upon them, and be obliged to acknowledge it; the witnesses note God saying the Egyptians should know he is the LORD when he stretched out his hand and brought Israel out — the great end of the plagues, that Egypt should be made to know Jehovah (God naming the purpose of all the judgments: 'the Egyptians shall know that I am the LORD, when I stretch forth mine hand upon Egypt, and bring out the children of Israel from among them'; the great design, 'the Egyptians shall know that I am the LORD,' that the plagues were not blind vengeance but had this end, that proud Egypt, with all her gods, should be forced to know and own Jehovah, the one only true and living God; the means, 'when I stretch forth mine hand upon Egypt,' that by his mighty acts of judgment, his outstretched hand, God would make himself known, even to those who said, 'Who is the LORD?'; the double work, 'and bring out the children of Israel from among them,' that the same hand that judged Egypt should deliver Israel, both the judgment and the salvation declaring who God is; that God will be known, whether men own him willingly or are forced to it by his judgments).

themes: the Egyptians shall know that I am the LORD
when I stretch forth mine hand upon Egypt, and bring out the children of Israel from among them
the great end of the plagues, that Egypt should know Jehovah the one true God
the same outstretched hand judging Egypt and delivering Israel, both declaring who God is

schools.summary: Gill: after this they never showed any reluctance or made any objection to any message they were sent with, but did as the Lord commanded; the witnesses note Moses and Aaron doing as the LORD commanded them, so did they — the prompt and full obedience of God's servants, their objections now past (the brothers obeying without further demur: 'Moses and Aaron did as the LORD commanded them, so did they'; the change in Moses, that, having at first raised objection after objection, he now, fully assured and commissioned, 'did as the LORD commanded,' and after this never showed any reluctance nor objected to any message; the fulness of the obedience, 'so did they,' the repetition marking that they did exactly, fully, and without reserve what God required; the pattern of true obedience, to do all that God commands, and to do it as he commands it; that God's servants, once their fears are answered by his promises, go forward in ready obedience to the hardest tasks).

themes: And Moses and Aaron did as the LORD commanded them
so did they
the prompt and full obedience of Moses and Aaron, their objections now past
the pattern of true obedience, to do all God commands, and as he commands it

schools.summary: Gill: Moses was eighty years old at this time, observed partly to show how long Israel had been afflicted in Egypt, their great troubles having begun about the time of his birth; the witnesses note Moses being fourscore years old, and Aaron fourscore and three, when they spoke to Pharaoh — the noting of their advanced age, that the great work was given to old men (the historian marking how old the deliverers were: 'Moses was fourscore years old, and Aaron fourscore and three years old, when they spake unto Pharaoh'; the ages, that Moses was eighty and Aaron eighty-three, both old men, when they began the great work of confronting Pharaoh and leading out Israel; the use of the note, that it shows the long affliction of Israel, the troubles having begun about Moses' birth, and that God reserved Moses for forty years in Pharaoh's court and forty in the wilderness, fitting him through long preparation; the encouragement the commentators draw, that God can fit and use his servants in old age, the great deliverance wrought by men of fourscore years, that the power might plainly be of God and not of man's vigour; that God's times are not ours, and his best instruments are often long in the making).

themes: And Moses was fourscore years old, and Aaron fourscore and three years old
when they spake unto Pharaoh
the great work given to old men, Moses eighty and Aaron eighty-three
God using his servants in old age, that the power might plainly be of God

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: here begins the attestation of the divine mission of Moses and Aaron, by Jehovah's directions, as they went to Pharaoh and proved their commission by a sign; Gill: this came after their commission and a little before they went in to him; the witnesses note the LORD speaking to Moses and Aaron, a little before they went in to Pharaoh — the further instruction of the messengers, preparing them for the demand of a sign (God adding direction for the meeting with the king: 'the LORD spake unto Moses and unto Aaron, saying'; the occasion, that this came after he had given them their commission and a little before they went in to him, God furnishing them with all they should need; the gracious care of God for his servants, that he did not send them unprepared but instructed them beforehand what to do when Pharaoh should challenge them; that what follows is the attestation of their divine mission, the sign by which they should prove they came from God; that God goes before his servants in every step of his work, leaving them not to their own counsel).

themes: And the LORD spake unto Moses and unto Aaron
saying
God's further instruction before the meeting with Pharaoh
the gracious care of God, who goes before his servants in every step

schools.summary: Gill: Pharaoh would require a miracle to prove they came from God, the Jehovah they said they did, hoping they would not be able to perform it; the witnesses note God telling them that, when Pharaoh demanded a miracle, Aaron should cast down his rod and it should become a serpent — the appointed sign to attest their divine commission (God foretelling the challenge and the answer: 'When Pharaoh shall speak unto you, saying, Shew a miracle for you: then... cast it before Pharaoh, and it shall become a serpent'; the foreseen demand, 'Shew a miracle for you,' that Pharaoh, on reflection, would ask a sign to prove their claim to come from the Jehovah they said they did, perhaps hoping they could give none; the appointed answer, 'Take thy rod, and cast it before Pharaoh, and it shall become a serpent,' the rod cast down and turned into a serpent, a sign of God's power; the fitness of a sign to attest a mission, that God confirms the word of his messengers by works beyond man's power; that God gave the very sign Pharaoh would seek, that his unbelief might be without excuse).

themes: Shew a miracle for you
Take thy rod, and cast it before Pharaoh, and it shall become a serpent
God foretelling Pharaoh's demand and appointing the rod-serpent sign
God confirming his messengers by works beyond man's power, that unbelief be without excuse

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: the word rendered 'serpent' is tannin, and learned men are not agreed what kind of serpent is meant; Gill: they went in boldly, clothed with power and authority and assured of success; the witnesses note Moses and Aaron going boldly in to Pharaoh, doing as God commanded, Aaron casting down his rod, and it becoming a serpent — the bold obedience of the messengers and the working of the sign (the brothers doing as bidden and the wonder wrought: 'Aaron cast down his rod before Pharaoh, and before his servants, and it became a serpent'; the boldness, that Moses and Aaron went in unto Pharaoh with courage, clothed with such power and authority and assured of success, not shrinking before the king; the exact obedience, 'they did so as the LORD had commanded,' doing the thing precisely as appointed; the sign itself, 'Aaron cast down his rod... and it became a serpent,' the rod turned, before Pharaoh and his court, into a living serpent, the word being tannin of uncertain kind, a real and visible wonder; that God thus attested his messengers before the king and his servants, openly and undeniably).

themes: Aaron cast down his rod before Pharaoh, and before his servants, and it became a serpent
they did so as the LORD had commanded
Moses and Aaron going boldly in, the rod becoming a serpent
a real and visible wonder, God openly attesting his messengers before Pharaoh

schools.summary: Gill: these were the cunning men and wizards, a sort of jugglers and deceivers, who pretended to great knowledge, to discover secrets and do wonderful things by legerdemain tricks and casting a mist before people's eyes; the witnesses note Pharaoh calling the wise men, sorcerers, and magicians of Egypt, who did in like manner with their enchantments — the counterfeit of the magicians, set against the true sign of God (Pharaoh seeking to match the wonder by his own sorcerers: 'Pharaoh also called the wise men and the sorcerers... they also did in like manner with their enchantments'; who they were, 'the wise men and the sorcerers,' the magicians of Egypt, cunning men and wizards, jugglers and deceivers who pretended to great knowledge and to do wonders by legerdemain tricks and enchantments; that Pharaoh, unwilling to yield, called them to oppose the sign, hoping to show that Moses' wonder was no more than his own men could do; the warning the commentators draw, that Satan and his agents counterfeit the works of God, to harden men in unbelief and turn them from the truth; that the powers of darkness ever set themselves against the work of God, by imitation and deceit).

themes: Then Pharaoh also called the wise men and the sorcerers
they also did in like manner with their enchantments
Pharaoh's magicians counterfeiting the sign by their enchantments
a warning that Satan and his agents imitate the works of God to harden men in unbelief

schools.summary: Gill: they seemed to become serpents, as Josephus expresses it, but not really, in which he is followed by many, though some think the devil assisted, removing the rods and putting real serpents in their place; the witnesses note the magicians casting down their rods, which became serpents, but Aaron's rod swallowing up theirs — the great proof of God's superiority, his sign devouring the counterfeit (the contest decided in God's favour: 'they cast down every man his rod, and they became serpents: but Aaron's rod swallowed up their rods'; the question much debated, whether the magicians' rods really became serpents or only seemed so, some with Josephus and Gill holding it a deception of sight, a juggling trick, others holding that the devil, by God's permission, did somewhat, removing the rods and substituting real serpents; that, whatever the means, the magicians' work was at best a counterfeit, far short of the true miracle; the decisive sign, 'Aaron's rod swallowed up their rods,' that God's serpent devoured the serpents of the sorcerers, an open and overwhelming proof that the power of God is above all the powers of magic and darkness; the comfort, that whatever Satan can counterfeit, God's truth swallows up and prevails over the lie).

debates.question: Did the magicians' rods really become serpents, or only seem to?

sides.position: Only seemed so — a deception of sight or juggling trick (a counterfeit far short of the true miracle)
The devil, by God's permission, did somewhat — removing the rods and substituting real serpents

themes: they cast down every man his rod, and they became serpents
but Aaron's rod swallowed up their rods
God's sign swallowing up the magicians' counterfeit
the proof that God's power is above all the powers of magic and darkness, truth swallowing the lie

schools.summary: Gill: it may be read, 'notwithstanding the heart of Pharaoh was hardened,' though he saw the rods of his magicians devoured, or 'therefore' his heart was hardened, because he saw they became serpents as well as Aaron's; the witnesses note Pharaoh's heart being hardened so that he hearkened not, as the LORD had said — the hardening confirmed and the word fulfilled, despite the open sign (Pharaoh, against all evidence, hardening himself: 'he hardened Pharaoh's heart, that he hearkened not unto them; as the LORD had said'; the reading of the verse, that Pharaoh's heart was hardened even notwithstanding he had seen Aaron's rod swallow the magicians' rods, a sign that should have moved him, or that his heart was hardened because he saw the magicians do somewhat like and so excused his unbelief; the result, 'that he hearkened not unto them,' that, hardened, he refused to heed the demand or regard the sign; the fulfilment, 'as the LORD had said,' that this very hardening had been foretold, so that even Pharaoh's obstinacy confirmed God's word; the dreadful truth, that signs alone do not soften a heart set against God, and that the hardening, his own sin, yet fell out as God had said).

themes: And he hardened Pharaoh's heart, that he hearkened not unto them
as the LORD had said
Pharaoh hardened against the open sign, refusing to hearken
signs alone do not soften a heart set against God; his obstinacy fell out as God foretold

schools.summary: Gill: Pharaoh's heart was 'heavy,' dull and stupid, stiff and inflexible, so that he could not lift up his heart or find in his heart to obey the will of God; the witnesses note the LORD telling Moses that Pharaoh's heart was hardened and he refused to let the people go — the renewed sentence on Pharaoh's obstinacy, the ground of the plague to follow (God naming the hardness that calls forth judgment: 'Pharaoh's heart is hardened, he refuseth to let the people go'; the description of the hardness, 'Pharaoh's heart is hardened,' or 'heavy,' that his heart was dull and stupid, stiff and inflexible, so set against God that he could not find in his heart to obey; the open refusal, 'he refuseth to let the people go,' that, having seen the sign, he still would not yield, his obstinacy now plain and stubborn; that God notes this hardness to Moses as the reason for the plagues now to begin, the judgment falling on a heart that would not bend to the word; that the more a man hardens himself against God, the heavier the judgment he draws upon himself).

themes: And the LORD said unto Moses, Pharaoh's heart is hardened
he refuseth to let the people go
Pharaoh's heart heavy, dull and inflexible, refusing to yield
the obstinacy that calls forth the plagues, judgment falling on a heart that would not bend

schools.summary: Gill: Moses was to go in the morning, when the mind is most composed and sedate and fit to attend to what may be suggested; the witnesses note God bidding Moses meet Pharaoh in the morning at the river, with the rod that had become a serpent in his hand — the appointed meeting and the instrument of judgment (God directing the time, place, and means: 'Get thee unto Pharaoh in the morning; lo, he goeth out unto the water... and the rod which was turned to a serpent shalt thou take in thine hand'; the time, 'in the morning,' when the mind is most composed and sedate, fit to receive the message, and the place, 'by the river's brink,' where Pharaoh goeth out unto the water; the reason some give for Pharaoh's going to the river, that he went down to the Nile, which Egypt worshipped, for his morning devotions or for pleasure, so that the plague should fall on the very god he honoured; the instrument, 'the rod which was turned to a serpent shalt thou take in thine hand,' the same rod that had wrought the sign, now to smite the waters, that Pharaoh might know the same power was at work; God's exact ordering of all the circumstances of his judgments).

themes: Get thee unto Pharaoh in the morning; lo, he goeth out unto the water
the rod which was turned to a serpent shalt thou take in thine hand
God sending Moses to meet Pharaoh at the river with the rod
the plague to fall on the Nile, the very god Egypt worshipped; God's exact ordering of every circumstance

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: the name 'Jehovah, the God of the Hebrews' is used; on the first application Pharaoh had professed not to know Jehovah and is now to be taught; the witnesses note Moses charged to tell Pharaoh that the LORD God of the Hebrews had sent him, demanding that his people be let go to serve him, and that hitherto Pharaoh would not hear — the persistent demand and the charge of disobedience (God renewing his claim and noting Pharaoh's refusal: 'The LORD God of the Hebrews hath sent me unto thee, saying, Let my people go... and, behold, hitherto thou wouldest not hear'; the authority claimed, 'The LORD God of the Hebrews hath sent me,' that Moses comes in the name of Jehovah, the God whom Pharaoh had said he knew not, and is now to be taught; the constant demand, 'Let my people go, that they may serve me in the wilderness,' the same just requirement, that Israel be freed to worship their God; the charge laid, 'hitherto thou wouldest not hear,' that Pharaoh's past refusals are set before him, his guilt mounting, so that the coming plague should be the just answer to his disobedience; the patience of God, who warns before he strikes).

themes: The LORD God of the Hebrews hath sent me unto thee, saying, Let my people go
and, behold, hitherto thou wouldest not hear
the renewed demand in the name of the LORD God of the Hebrews
the patience of God who warns before he strikes, Pharaoh's guilt mounting

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: here commences the account of the ten plagues which were inflicted on the Egyptians; the witnesses note God declaring that, by smiting the river to blood, Pharaoh should know he is the LORD — the announcement of the first plague, the proof of God's power against the river-god (God naming the sign that should teach Pharaoh: 'In this thou shalt know that I am the LORD: behold, I will smite... upon the waters which are in the river, and they shall be turned to blood'; the design, 'In this thou shalt know that I am the LORD,' that the plague should be a proof of God's power and vengeance, teaching Pharaoh, who said he knew not the LORD, that Jehovah is God indeed; the act, 'I will smite with the rod... upon the waters which are in the river, and they shall be turned to blood,' that the very Nile, the life and the boast of Egypt, the river they worshipped, should be smitten and turned to blood, its waters made loathsome; that this first of the ten plagues struck at the heart of Egypt's pride and idolatry, judging the river-god and showing the true God's dominion over it; that God begins his judgments where Egypt's confidence lay).

debates.question: Why does the first plague fall on the river Nile?

sides.position: Because the Nile was the life, boast, and very god of Egypt — worshipped as divine; smiting it to blood struck at the heart of Egypt's pride and idolatry, judging the river-god and showing the true God's dominion over it

themes: In this thou shalt know that I am the LORD
I will smite... upon the waters which are in the river, and they shall be turned to blood
God announcing the first plague, the Nile turned to blood
the judgment striking at the heart of Egypt's pride and idolatry, judging the river-god

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: the force of the expression, that the Egyptians should 'loathe' to drink of the water, marks the greatness of the calamity; the witnesses note God foretelling that the fish should die, the river stink, and the Egyptians loathe to drink the water — the loathsome and grievous effects of the first plague (God showing how dreadful the judgment should be: 'the fish that is in the river shall die, and the river shall stink; and the Egyptians shall lothe to drink of the water of the river'; the death of the fish, 'the fish that is in the river shall die,' their element changed to blood, they could not live, and a chief food of Egypt destroyed; the corruption, 'the river shall stink,' that the bloodied waters should grow foul and noisome, the pleasant Nile become a stench; the deep distress, 'the Egyptians shall lothe to drink of the water,' that they should so abhor the bloody, stinking water that they could not bear to drink it, yet thirst for want of it, the very river they boasted in turned to their torment; that God can turn a nation's chief blessing into its plague, and its god into its grief).

themes: the fish that is in the river shall die, and the river shall stink
and the Egyptians shall lothe to drink of the water of the river
the grievous effects: fish dying, river stinking, water loathsome
God turning a nation's chief blessing into its plague, and its god into its grief

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: there should be blood both in vessels of wood and in vessels of stone, not only the Nile but all the waters of Egypt; the witnesses note God bidding Aaron stretch out the rod over all the waters of Egypt, that they become blood, even in vessels of wood and stone — the breadth of the plague, the whole land's water turned to blood (God extending the judgment to every water: 'stretch out thine hand upon the waters of Egypt... that they may become blood... throughout all the land of Egypt, both in vessels of wood, and in vessels of stone'; the command to Aaron, 'Take thy rod, and stretch out thine hand upon the waters of Egypt,' over their streams, rivers, ponds, and pools, the whole network of Egypt's waters; the universality, 'that there may be blood throughout all the land of Egypt,' not the Nile only but all the waters, even those already drawn and stored 'in vessels of wood, and in vessels of stone,' so that there should be no escape, no clean water anywhere; the completeness of the judgment, leaving Egypt wholly without water to drink, the plague reaching into every house; the power of God, who could turn all the water of a land to blood at the stretching of a rod).

themes: stretch out thine hand upon the waters of Egypt... that they may become blood
both in vessels of wood, and in vessels of stone
the plague extended to all Egypt's waters, even stored in vessels
the completeness of the judgment, no clean water anywhere, the power of God over all

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: all the waters were turned to blood, not merely in appearance but in reality; the witnesses note Moses and Aaron doing as commanded, Aaron lifting the rod and smiting the river before Pharaoh, so that all its waters became blood — the open execution of the judgment in the sight of the king (the word fulfilled before Pharaoh's eyes: 'he lifted up the rod, and smote the waters... in the sight of Pharaoh... and all the waters that were in the river were turned to blood'; the obedience, 'Moses and Aaron did so, as the LORD commanded,' Moses giving the rod to Aaron who smote the waters as appointed; the openness, 'in the sight of Pharaoh, and in the sight of his servants,' that the wonder was wrought publicly, before the king and his court, that none might doubt it; the reality, 'all the waters... were turned to blood,' not merely in appearance but in reality, a true and total change; that God performed exactly what he had threatened, his word and his work agreeing; that the same God who warns surely executes, his judgments certain).

themes: he lifted up the rod, and smote the waters that were in the river, in the sight of Pharaoh
all the waters that were in the river were turned to blood
the plague executed openly before Pharaoh and his court
all the waters truly turned to blood, not in appearance only but in reality

schools.summary: Gill: the death of the fish was a full proof that the conversion into blood was real, for had it been only in appearance the fish would not have died; the witnesses note the fish dying, the river stinking, the Egyptians unable to drink, and blood throughout all the land — the full effect of the plague, proving its reality and the greatness of the distress (all that was foretold now coming to pass: 'the fish that was in the river died; and the river stank, and the Egyptians could not drink... and there was blood throughout all the land of Egypt'; the proof of reality, 'the fish that was in the river died,' that the death of the fish showed the change was no mere colour or appearance but a true turning to blood, in which the fish could not live; the corruption and distress, 'the river stank, and the Egyptians could not drink of the water,' the foul stench and the want of drinkable water bringing real misery on the whole land; the universality, 'there was blood throughout all the land of Egypt,' the plague reaching everywhere, exactly as God had said; the agreement of the event with the word, that God's threat was fulfilled to the letter).

themes: the fish that was in the river died; and the river stank, and the Egyptians could not drink
and there was blood throughout all the land of Egypt
the full effect of the plague, the dead fish proving the change real
the event agreeing with the word, God's threat fulfilled to the letter

schools.summary: Gill: the magicians were either in company with Pharaoh and his nobles, or were sent for to try their art and confront Moses and Aaron with it; the witnesses note the magicians doing the like with their enchantments, and Pharaoh's heart being hardened so that he hearkened not, as the LORD had said — the counterfeit that hardened Pharaoh, the word again fulfilled (the magicians' imitation giving Pharaoh excuse to harden himself: 'the magicians of Egypt did so with their enchantments: and Pharaoh's heart was hardened... as the LORD had said'; the counterfeit, 'the magicians... did so with their enchantments,' that they turned some water to blood, whether by trick or by finding some clean water left or some show, seeming to match the wonder, though they could not undo it nor cleanse the river; the effect on Pharaoh, that 'Pharaoh's heart was hardened,' seizing on the magicians' show as a pretext, he hardened himself the more and hearkened not unto them; the folly and pride, that he took comfort from a counterfeit that could add to the plague but not remove it, his heart set on not yielding; the fulfilment, 'as the LORD had said,' that God's word concerning the hardening stood).

themes: the magicians of Egypt did so with their enchantments
Pharaoh's heart was hardened, neither did he hearken unto them; as the LORD had said
the magicians' counterfeit giving Pharaoh a pretext to harden himself
the folly of taking comfort from a counterfeit that could add to the plague but not remove it

schools.summary: Gill: Pharaoh turned away from Moses and Aaron, and turned back from the river, and went to his palace, not setting his heart to this either; the witnesses note Pharaoh turning away and going into his house, not setting his heart to the plague — the careless contempt of Pharaoh, turning his back on the warning (the king dismissing the judgment as a thing of no weight: 'Pharaoh turned, and went into his house, neither did he set his heart to this also'; the action, 'Pharaoh turned, and went into his house,' that he turned away from Moses and Aaron and from the bloodied river, and went home, as if nothing had happened; the careless contempt, 'neither did he set his heart to this also,' that he gave no serious thought to the plague, did not lay it to heart nor consider its meaning but put it from his mind; the addition 'to this also,' that, as he had slighted the former sign, so now he slighted this greater judgment, hardening in his neglect; the great danger of not laying God's judgments to heart, the careless contempt that ripens into ruin).

themes: And Pharaoh turned, and went into his house
neither did he set his heart to this also
Pharaoh's careless contempt, giving the plague no serious thought
the great danger of not laying God's judgments to heart, the contempt that ripens into ruin

schools.summary: Gill: all the Egyptians digged on each side of the river to get clear water, and Aben Ezra thinks the Hebrews also were affected with this and the two following plagues, the frogs and lice; the witnesses note the Egyptians digging round about the river for water, since they could not drink of the river — the misery of the people driven to dig for water, the proof of the plague's grievousness (the whole land set to digging for a drink: 'all the Egyptians digged round about the river for water to drink; for they could not drink of the water of the river'; the distress, that 'all the Egyptians digged round about the river,' labouring on every side to find some water fit to drink, perhaps water filtered through the soil and so cleaner; the cause, 'for they could not drink of the water of the river,' the river-water being turned to loathsome blood, undrinkable, so that the people were driven to this hard shift for the necessity of life; the greatness of the plague, that a whole nation should be reduced to digging in the sand for water, their proud river become useless; the comment some make, whether the Hebrews too were touched by this plague or were spared; that God can make the most needful and common thing fail, to humble the proud).

themes: And all the Egyptians digged round about the river for water to drink
for they could not drink of the water of the river
the whole land driven to dig for water, the river undrinkable
God making the most needful and common thing fail, to humble the proud

schools.summary: Gill: there were full seven days, a whole week, after the LORD had smitten the river and turned it into blood, and here the miracle is ascribed to him; the witnesses note that seven full days were fulfilled after the LORD had smitten the river — the duration of the first plague, a whole week of judgment, ascribed to God himself (the affliction measured out by God: 'seven days were fulfilled, after that the LORD had smitten the river'; the length, 'seven days... a whole week,' that the plague of blood lasted seven full days, a long and grievous time for the land to be without clean water; the ascription, 'after that the LORD had smitten the river,' that the Scripture here lays the act to God himself, 'the LORD had smitten the river,' though it was done by Aaron's rod, so that the glory and the authorship of the judgment are God's; the patience mingled with judgment, that God gave Pharaoh seven days' space, time to relent, before the next plague; that God measures both the length and the end of his judgments, none falling by chance).

themes: And seven days were fulfilled
after that the LORD had smitten the river
the first plague lasting seven days, ascribed to God himself
the patience mingled with judgment, giving Pharaoh space to relent before the next plague
